note: Saṅkhapāla Jātaka; the nāga (Saṅkhapāla) comes to the man, addressed janinda; passing narrative mention

claim: The hunters (luddaputtaka) are said to be foes/predators of the nāgas (verino nāma) who hunt them for food
note: Saṅkhapāla Jātaka

claim: Asks how the nāga (Saṅkhapāla) obtained his celestial palace/vimāna and divine pleasures
note: Saṅkhapāla; nāgassidaṃ = this of the nāga

claim: Gloss: by what karma did Saṅkhapāla-nāgarāja obtain his attainments and best vimāna
note: explicit nāgarāja gloss; karma-cause query

claim: Asks the nāga why, endowed with strength/beauty/wisdom, he practises ascetic observance (tapo)
note: Saṅkhapāla keeping uposatha as a nāga
Saṅkhapāla; the man takes leave of the nāga (janinda); passing mention
Saṅkhapāla addressed nāga; affectionate speech, passing mention
verse: having heard the nāga (Saṅkhapāla) and Āḷāra; passing
gloss naming nāgarājānaṃ (Saṅkhapāla) the diligent one; passing reference
verse variant: having heard the nāga; passing
King Sutasoma gone forth wanders alone like a nāga; simile of solitary great one applied to a monk-king, na the serpent realm; polysemy
punāgamissati = puna + āgamissati (will come again); not nāga morpheme
anāgāriyabhāvaṃ = the homeless state; an-agāra, not nāga
eating the flesh of the nāga (elephant), drinking Ganges water — a bird/vulture scene; nāgassa maṃsāni = elephant carcass
nāgañchi = na + agañchi/ajjhagā (did not reach/obtain a footing); gloss equates to na ajjhagā; not nāga
60,000 nāgas = elephants, adorned mātaṅgā with golden girths; army/parade elephants
like a forest nāga (āraññaka nāga) a calf (poto) follows behind over mountains; elephant simile
the nāgas adorned with head-ornaments, trained by hatthācariya (elephant-trainers); elephants
roars in pain like a nāga pricked by goads (tutta); goaded elephant simile
gloss: a nāga pierced by goads (tutta) roars; elephant
these nāgas stand armoured (vammitā), will trample the rampart (pākāra); war-elephants
gloss: until these nāgas trample the rampart; war-elephants
who has thousands of nāgas always yoked (yojenti); harnessed elephants
a mighty khattiya nāga disguised in frog-colour (maṇḍūkavaṇṇa); nāga = great bull-elephant/noble in martial verse, paired with khattiyaṃ mahabbalaṃ; elephant sense

buddhanāga (epithet of Buddha) vs tiracchānanāga (the animal nāga Nāḷāgiri = elephant); the referent of the relevant token-pair is the elephant Nāḷāgiri; buddhanāga is honorific. Coded elephant for the dominant animal sense
kuñjara! do not attack the nāga (Nāḷāgiri elephant addressed re Buddha); nāgamāsado/nāgahatassa about striking the elephant-nāga; elephant context (Nāḷāgiri)
bandhanāgāre = bandhana-agāra (prison); not nāga
nāgarā = the townsfolk/citizens, terrified; bandhanāgāre = prison (nonlexical) but coding the citizen token
nāgabalo = with elephant-strength (simile-epithet of Mahāsatta, like a tusker freed); nāgarā = citizens. The lead nāga-token (nāgabalo) is an elephant-strength epithet of a person; coded epithet
city furnished with horses, nāgas (elephants), chariots (hayehi nāgehi rathehi); elephants

claim: One makes bali-offerings to nāgas and yakkhas at sea-pools, ponds and rivers (their watery haunts)
note: nāgayakkhādīnaṃ balikammaṃ; nāgas as recipients of offerings dwelling in waters
nānāgāhaṃ = nānā + gāhaṃ (diverse contention/quarrel); not nāga
nānāgāhaṃ = nānā-gāhaṃ (varied dispute); not nāga
horses like young nāgas (susunāga-sādise); equine simile to elephants; elephant sense of nāga
gloss: susunāgasādise = like young nāgas in strength (taruṇanāga-sadise); elephant
sace satthā nāgamissa = na + āgamissa (if the Teacher had not come); not nāga
long compound: ...mahāvarāha-nāga-kula-kareṇu...; nāga in a forest-fauna list, glossed (next row) as mahāhatthino; elephant
gloss: nāgakulena = herd of elephants (mahāhatthino) with cow-elephants (kareṇu); elephant
long flower/tree compound containing nāgarukkha and punnāga in a list of forest trees; tree
nāgabalo mahāsatto = the elephant-strong Bodhisatta; simile-epithet of a person; coded epithet
nāgami = na + āgami (did not come); not nāga
nāgamissatī = na + āgamissati (his line will not continue); not nāga; Nimi Jātaka frame
anāgārā tapassino = homeless ascetics; an-agāra, not nāga
anāgārā tapassino = homeless ascetics; an-agāra, not nāga
nāgabalo bodhisatto = elephant-strong Bodhisatta (child); simile-epithet of a person; epithet
Mahosadha verse: gift of a thousand cows, a bull and a nāgaṃ (elephant), yoked chariots; elephant
gloss: usabhañca nāgaṃ = a bull-elephant adorned and rideable (ārohanīyaṃ nāgaṃ); elephant
bandhanāgārāni = prisons (bandhana-agāra); not nāga
nāgamissanti = na + āgamissanti (they will not come); not nāga
yoke horses to chariots, bind the girth on the nāgas (kacchaṃ nāgāna bandhatha); harnessing elephants for battle
Mahosadha; 'there are sky-going nāgas with iddhi' but glossed next row as hatthino (chaddanta/uposatha-lineage elephants); elephant, so claim_bearing false per codebook
explicit gloss nāgā ti hatthino = elephants; sky-going chaddanta/uposatha-kula elephants; elephant, claim_bearing false per codebook
mounting a fine nāga, mighty, sixty years old (saṭṭhihāyana); war-elephant
mahānāgā = great heroes/warriors (māṇavā sūrā) set against the elephants (hatthīnaṃ sammukhā); mahānāga here = great-men epithet of warriors; epithet
nāgā ca kappitā dantī = tusked nāgas caparisoned, sixty years old, on whose shoulders princes shine; war-elephants
nāgakhandhesu = on the elephants' shoulders the men shine; elephant
archers swooping down onto elephant-shoulders (nāgakhandhe nipātino); elephant
mahānāgā glossed as warriors who stand firm and seize the tusks of charging elephants; mahānāga = great-warrior epithet (distinct from the mahānāgesu = great elephants in same passage); coded epithet
gloss: nāgakhandhe nipātino = able to cut down with sword onto elephant-shoulders (hatthikkhandhe); elephant
goading the nāgaṃ with the vajira-goad (vajiraṅkusa); war-elephant
why do you hurriedly send the nāga, the kuñjara (elephant)? nāgaṃ pesesi kuñjaraṃ; elephant
the leopard is slain for its skin, the nāga is slain for its tusks (dantehi); elephant
nānāgāhaṃ gaṇhāti = takes up varied contention (nānā-gāhaṃ); not nāga
nānāgāhaṃ gaṇhāti = takes up varied contention; not nāga

manussapathaṃ nāgami = na + āgami (came back to the human path... actually puna...nāgami = na āgami? context: returned -> 'puna manussapathaṃ nāgami' reads na+āgami 'did not come'? treated as na+āgami false friend); not nāga

claim: The king reasons: if a deva, he will fly up; if a nāga, he will sink into the earth — distinguishing nāga by its earth-entering power
note: Sāma/related Jātaka frame; nāga contrasted with deva and manussa as a class of being with the power to enter earth
I am skilled in archery; even a nāga would not escape me; a hunter's boast — nāga = elephant (game), paired with daḷhadhamma archery; elephant

claim: Gloss: both devas and nāgas speak in human language; the king cannot tell if the being is deva, nāga, or human
note: devāpi nāgāpi manussabhāsāya kathenti; nāga as a class of being able to speak and shapeshift, contrasted with deva/human

claim: Lists deva, nāga, kinnara, khattiya etc. as kinds of being one might claim to be
note: devanāgakinnarakhattiyādīsu; nāga enumerated as a class of being
the leopard slain for skin, the nāga slain for its tusks (dantehi); elephant
natthi maccussa nāgamo = na + āgamo (there is no not-coming of death / death's coming is inevitable); gloss maccussa āgamanaṃ; not nāga
nāgamo glossed maccussa āgamanaṃ = death's coming; na+āgamo; not nāga
sīha-byaggha-dīpi-yakkha-nāga-kinnarānaṃ: nāga listed among beings (lion, tiger, leopard, yakkha, nāga, kinnara) whose footstep it is not; passing list-mention, no ontological claim
duplicate of p868: even a nāga would not escape me, says the archer; elephant (game)
nānāgandhasamoditaṃ = nānā-gandha (various scents); not nāga; Vessantara forest description
nāgamallikā = nāga-jasmine creeper, in a list of flowering plants (koraṇḍaka, anojā, kiṃsuka); tree/plant
gloss: nāgamallikā = vallināgā ca mallikā ca (creeper-nāga and jasmine), flowering plants; tree/plant

birds and nāgas calling to one another in a list with cocks, herons (kukkuṭā nāgā ... bakā balākā); among birds — but nāga as serpent/creature in a fauna list; passing mention. (Could be a bird-name but the standard list-of-beings reading is serpent.)
a forest frequented by nāgas (nāgasaṃsevitaṃ vanaṃ), strewn with deer; nāgas haunting the wood; passing scenery mention of serpent-beings
nānāgandhehi bhūsite = adorned with various scents (nānā-gandha); not nāga

claim: The nāga-clans (nāgakulāni) that guard the lotus-lake are honoured/saluted as resident deities of the water
note: Vessantara; nāgakulāni as guardian deities of the pond, bowed to with the head
their footprints like nāgas' on a mountain (nāgānamiva pabbate); gloss (next) = elephants' tracks (padavalañja) in the rains; elephant
gloss: nāgānaṃ padavalañjaṃ = elephants' footprints on the mountain-slope in the rains; elephant
in whose house there is no slave, horse, mule-chariot, hatthī, kuñjara, nāga; nāga in a list with hatthī/kuñjara = elephant
yoke fourteen thousand nāgas, golden-girthed mātaṅgā; elephants (mātaṅga gloss)
gloss: that nāga (the elephant, so hatthī) brought and given to Sañjaya, trumpeted (koñcanāda); explicit hatthī; elephant
mounting the nāga Paccaya (the elephant named Paccaya); glossed next as hatthināga; elephant
gloss: paccayaṃ nāgamāruyha = mounting the hatthināga (elephant) Paccaya born on his birth-day; explicit hatthināga; elephant
they brought her a nāga, a not-too-large kuñjara (elephant); elephant
Maddī mounted the nāga, a not-too-large kuñjara; elephant
gloss: nāgamāruhi = mounted the back of a fine elephant (varahatthi-piṭṭhiṃ); explicit hatthi; elephant

claim: Bhūridatta frame: Dhataraṭṭha the nāga-king; nāga-youths (nāgamāṇavaka) leave the nāga-realm (nāgabhavana) taking a tortoise; the nāga king arranges marriage of his daughter Samuddajā
note: Bhūridatta Jātaka opening; nāgarāja, nāgabhavana, nāgamāṇavaka — establishes the nāga realm and society

claim: We have never made a marriage-alliance with nāgas before; how shall we make such an ill-matched marriage?
note: Bhūridatta; humans reluctant to intermarry with nāgas — asserts nāgas as a distinct (and other) class of being

claim: When a nāga (Dhataraṭṭha) is angered, such men do not live long
note: Bhūridatta; the deadly power of an enraged nāga-king

claim: Gloss: when so mighty a nāga (Varuṇa-nāgarāja) is angered men cannot live long, they perish; identifies Varuṇa nāgarāja
note: Bhūridatta; explicit varuṇanāgarājassa; lethal power

claim: Dhataraṭṭha is overlord (issaro) of many nāgas
note: Bhūridatta; Dhataraṭṭha as ruler over the nāgas — nāga society/realm

claim: Let the Kambala and Assatara nāgas rise up; announce to all nāgas to overspread Bārāṇasī but harm no one
note: Bhūridatta; mobilising the nāga hosts; their power to invade a city

claim: Gloss: the Kambala-Assatara nāgas dwelling at the foot of Sineru (sinerupāde vasananāgā), the king's maternal kin, are summoned; their breath-blast can reduce the city to ash
note: Bhūridatta; nāga abodes at Sineru's foot, kin-groups, and venom/breath power
Bhūridatta; the nāgas ask what to do; narrative connective, no fresh ontological claim

claim: The nāgas, becoming huge-bodied (vitatasarīrā mahāsarīrā), hang from doorways and lie in the water and on land throughout the city
note: Bhūridatta; nāgas' power to enlarge their bodies and pervade the city
Bhūridatta; then all the nāgas did so; narrative connective

claim: Seeing the nāgas hanging coiled (soṇḍikate) and breathing/hissing again and again, the women wailed
note: Bhūridatta; nāgas with coils/hoods breathing — serpent form and menacing power

claim: The four nāga-youths (nāgamāṇavaka), as envoys, encircled the king's bed, made great hoods (phaṇa) over his head and stood with bared fangs hissing
note: Bhūridatta; explicit phaṇa (hood) and fangs — serpent form and intimidation

claim: At dawn, the city heaved as if upturned by the nāgas' breath-blast; the people cry 'nāga-kings, why do you harry us?'
note: Bhūridatta; nāgarājāno, nāga breath-power over the city

claim: Dhataraṭṭha vanished with his nāga-retinue and reappeared in the nāga-realm (nāgabhavana), a 500-yojana realm adorned like a deva-city with jewelled palaces
note: Bhūridatta; detailed description of the nāgabhavana as a splendid realm

claim: The nāginī (Samuddajā) bore four sons but did not know she was in the nāga-realm; the young nāga Ariṭṭha tests his mother by manifesting a serpent-body (sappasarīra) while suckling
note: Bhūridatta Uposathakaṇḍa; nāgas living as humans, reverting to serpent form; nāginī, taruṇanāgā

claim: Their father gave each son a hundred-yojana realm; thousands of nāga-maidens (nāgakaññāsahassāni) attend; Bhūridatta attends Virūpakkha and goes with the nāga-retinue to Tāvatiṃsa to serve Sakka
note: Bhūridatta; nāga society, realms, nāgakaññā, and nāgas visiting the deva-world

claim: Asks: are you a mighty yakkha, or are you a nāga of great power (nāgosi mahānubhāvo)?
note: Bhūridatta Garuḷakhaṇḍa; nāga enumerated as a powerful class of being alongside yakkha

claim: 'I am a nāga (nāgohamasmi), possessed of iddhi, fiery, hard to overcome; angered, I could blast even a prosperous country with my heat/venom (tejasā)'
note: Bhūridatta; first-person nāga self-description — iddhi and venom/fire power

claim: A garuḷa/supaṇṇa seized a nāga-king (nāgarājānaṃ) by the head from the sea and carried it off; the nāga wrapped its tail round a banyan to escape
note: Bhūridatta; supaṇṇa-nāga predation; explicit nāgarājā; the standard garuḷa-eats-nāga relation

claim: Gloss on the garuḷa carrying off the nāga for food (bhojanatthāya nāgaṃ haranto); discusses karma of seizing the nāga
note: Bhūridatta; supaṇṇa preys on nāga; predation relation
Bhūridatta Kīḷanakaṇḍa: 'whoever shows me the mahānāga, fiery, hard to overcome, to him I would give this gem'; refers to Bhūridatta the serpent but a passing offer, no ontological claim here

claim: Who in brahmin-guise, a supaṇṇa lord of birds, seeks to conquer the nāga, hunting his own prey (bhakkha)?
note: Bhūridatta; supaṇṇa hunting the nāga as prey (bhakkha); predation

Bhūridatta; gloss bhoginaṃ = nāgānaṃ (of the nāgas/coiled ones), visaghātānaṃ = venom-curing physicians; lexical gloss, refers to serpents but a definitional aside; no fresh ontological claim — but it does identify bhogin=nāga. Marked false (definitional, not soteriological)
Bhūridatta; the snake-charmer plots to get the nāga (Bhūridatta) shown to him for the jewel; passing narrative
Bhūridatta; 'seize that mahānāga, bring me the gem, red-headed, indagopaka-coloured'; describes the serpent's appearance but in a command, passing

claim: The gem fell to the ground and at once sank into the earth and went back to the nāga-realm (nāgabhavanaṃ)
note: Bhūridatta; the nāga (Bhūridatta) seized; the nāga-gem returns to the nāgabhavana through the earth

claim: Bhūridatta is too large for the basket; the charmer makes him dance; the nāga resolves to perform to please the crowd so as to be released
note: Bhūridatta; the captive nāga's reasoning and dance; serpent in captivity, body-size
bhūridatto ... nāgamissati = na + āgamissati (if Bhūridatta does not come tonight); not nāga
Bhūridatta; 'was such a nāga made to dance here by some snake-charmer?'; refers to Bhūridatta the serpent, passing inquiry

claim: The nāga-king (Bhūridatta) is summoned to dance; nāgas look over the crowd for two reasons — supaṇṇa-danger or kinsfolk; fearing supaṇṇa they will not dance, ashamed before kin they will not dance
note: Bhūridatta; explicit nāga behaviour/psychology (supaṇṇa-fear, kin-shame); nāgarājā
Bhūridatta; 'this nāga of mine is not enough to harm anyone'; the charmer boasting about the captive serpent; passing
Bhūridatta; wager: 'you with the nāga, I with the frog' (a contest of serpent venom vs frog); passing challenge
Bhūridatta; gloss of the wager: you fight with the nāga, I with the frog; passing
Bhūridatta; 'Alampāyana seeks to conquer me with the nāga, I will make the frog bite the brahmin'; passing
Bhūridatta; 'you slight this serpent (uraga), pay no homage to the nāga'; passing reproach, the nāga = Bhūridatta

claim: 'With a venomless nāga (avisena nāgena) you greatly deceive people' — implies an ordinary nāga would have venom
note: Bhūridatta; venom as a property of nāgas (here Bhūridatta keeps precepts so is 'venomless')
Bhūridatta; 'you who slight such a nāga as venomless'; passing reproach

claim: There may be venom in the silutta, deḍḍubha, silābhu (lesser snakes), but the red-headed nāga has none — comparing venom across snake-kinds
note: Bhūridatta; venom-bearing classification of the nāga vs lesser serpents

claim: 'This nāga is of great iddhi, fiery, hard to overcome; with it I will make him bite you, and it will reduce you to ash'
note: Bhūridatta; the nāga's fiery/venom power asserted

claim: When the Mahāsatta arrived the whole nāga-realm (nāgabhavana) was one cry of grief; countless nāgas come; a fierce nāga is set as doorkeeper to control the nāga-assembly
note: Bhūridatta; nāgabhavana, the nāga-assembly (nāgaparisā), nāga society

claim: Gloss: amarādhipa = lord of the nāgas, called 'deathless' (amara) because of their long life (dīghāyukatā)
note: Bhūridatta; explicit claim about nāga longevity — amarādhipa of the nāgas, dīghāyukatāya amarasaṅkhātānaṃ nāgānaṃ

claim: Gloss: puraṃ = the nāga-city (nāgapuraṃ), submerged (ogāḷha), near the Yamunā at the foot of Himavant where the nāga-brothers dwell
note: Bhūridatta; nāgapura, the submerged nāga-city beneath the Yamunā

claim: Kāṇāriṭṭha warns Subhoga not to harm the brahmins lest Mahābrahmā destroy the whole nāga-realm (nāgabhavana); addresses the nāga-assembly (nāgaparisā)
note: Bhūridatta Micchākathā; nāgabhavana, nāgaparisā; nāga society and its vulnerability
Bhūridatta; '...so that the nāga-assembly (nāgaparisā) might not get to say he wronged Bhūridatta'; passing reference to the assembly

claim: Many nāgas attending the sick Mahāsatta took up wrong view from Ariṭṭha's speech; the Mahāsatta resolves to break his argument and make the nāga-assembly right-viewed (sammādiṭṭhika)
note: Bhūridatta; nāgas as capable of holding right/wrong view and hearing/receiving Dhamma — nāgaparisā made sammādiṭṭhika

claim: The supaṇṇa-king: 'this nāga is my chief food (aggabhakkha), yet I forbear from killing it for food's sake' — nāga as the garuḷa's prey
note: Bhūridatta/related; nāga as the supaṇṇa's staple prey; predation relation
Vidhura/Mahosadha cross-quote: gift of a thousand cows, a bull and a nāgaṃ (elephant), yoked chariots; elephant
Vidhura Jātaka: Irandhatī addresses her father Varuṇa as nāgakuñjara (nāga-bull, honorific for the nāga-king); refers to the serpent-king, vocative; passing
Vidhura; gloss: janinda = lord of the nāga-folk (nāgajanassa inda), nāgakuñjara/nāgaseṭṭha = chief of nāgas; honorifics of Varuṇa the nāga-king; definitional, no fresh ontological claim

claim: 'Which gandhabbas, rakkhasas, nāgas, kimpurisas, or humans... who is the wise one?' — nāga enumerated among classes of being who might be a suitor/husband
note: Vidhura; nāga listed as a distinct class of being capable of marriage

claim: Gloss: 'which gandhabba or rakkhasa or nāga... which among them is wise enough to give all desires' — nāga as a class of being
note: Vidhura; nāga enumerated among beings
Vidhura; Puṇṇaka addresses the nāga-king (nāgavara, vocative 'best of nāgas') asking for Irandhatī; refers to Varuṇa the serpent; passing
Vidhura; 'a hundred elephants, horses... accept these, O nāga, and give your daughter'; vocative to the nāga-king; passing offer
Vidhura; 'then that Varuṇa the nāga, entering his dwelling, addressed his wife'; explicit varuṇo nāgo; passing narrative
Vidhura; 'then that Varuṇa the nāga, leaving his dwelling, addressed Puṇṇaka'; passing narrative
Vidhura; 'whom do you call wise, O nāga?'; vocative to the nāga-king; passing
Vidhura; Campeyyaka nāgamallikā, bhaginīmālā, koliyā — flowering trees adorning the serpent's mansion (uragassa mandiraṃ); nāgamallikā = nāga-jasmine plant; tree

claim: Describes the nāga-king's golden realm Bhogavatī: the nāga-king's (nāgarājā) dwelling-place, jewelled palaces, the nāga-realm (nāgabhavana) Hiraññavatī walled in gold
note: Vidhura; the nāgabhavana Bhogavatī described in detail; nāgarājā/nāgabhavana
pānāgāre = pāna-agāra (drinking-hall/tavern); not nāga
Bhūridatta; the Mahāsatta won the debate, the whole nāga-assembly (nāgaparisā) rejoiced; he had the brahmin removed from the nāga-realm (nāgabhavana); narrative wrap-up, no fresh claim
so ito sattame divase nāgamissati = na + āgamissati (if he does not come on the seventh day); not nāga
Vidhura; 'Varuṇa by name, a nāga, tall, bright, endowed with beauty and strength'; describes Varuṇa the nāga-king; passing identification

claim: 'With your Dhamma-won heart, the nāgas give you Irandhatī the nāga-maiden (nāgakaññā)' — the nāgas give a nāga-maiden in marriage
note: Vidhura; nāgakaññā given in marriage by the nāgas; nāga society/realm
Vidhura; 'let the nāga-clan's (nāgakulā) wealth perish, never mind the nāga-maiden (nāgakaññā)...'; refers to the serpent-clan/maiden; passing renunciatory speech
